ai_met_criteria: Pelvic pain >6 weeks documented as 'ongoing abdo pain' [p46]
ai_missing_criteria: Duration of pelvic pain not specified as >6 weeks [p46]
PID exclusion not documented [p46]

ai_notes: The referral mentions suspected endometriosis (?Endo) but ultrasound pelvis criteria specifically redirect suspected endometriosis without palpable mass to the Endometriosis HealthPathway rather than imaging. The pelvic pain pathway requires >6 weeks duration, PID exclusion, and is unrelated to menstrual cycle - none of these are clearly documented.
ai_redirect: Suspected endometriosis in the absence of a clinically palpable mass — see local Endometriosis HealthPathway
ai_suggested_wording: 32F with ongoing abdominal and pelvic pain for [specify duration >6 weeks]. Examination reveals soft abdomen with RIF and suprapubic tenderness, no guarding, no organomegaly. Request pelvic ultrasound to exclude other pelvic pathology.

clinical_note:
Neck and thyroid ultrasound 

80 year old female
2. pain in neck 
sharp pain - feels like something in poking through - C2/3 - where screws have been placed
requests referral back to Ortho
pain wakes her up in the night
occurs intermittently 
denies tingling or numbess
nil incontinence
nil cough or cold sx recently 
nil loss of sensation to genitals, anal area, groin

O/E
FROM - however feels it "catches me"
tenderness over palpation left side of C2/3
nil lump felt - however she has felt this
Has Xrays - area of interest is where screws have been placed
alert and comfortable

?imp dislodged screw ?

Plan:
1. Re-refer toOrtho + USS
2. Red flags adviced on when to present to ED

ai_verdict_summary: This presentation relates to cervical spine hardware complications, not thyroid or neck mass pathology covered by ultrasound neck/thyroid criteria.
ai_missing_criteria: No palpable thyroid nodule documented [p43]
No neck mass >1.5cm documented [p43]
No incidental thyroid findings on other imaging documented [p43]
ai_notes: The clinical presentation describes cervical spine hardware complications (suspected dislodged screw at C2/3 level) rather than thyroid or neck mass pathology. Ultrasound neck/thyroid is not the appropriate investigation for spinal hardware assessment. The GP's plan to re-refer to Orthopaedics is correct.
ai_redirect: Cervical spine hardware complications with night pain and suspected dislodged screw — refer directly to Orthopaedics as planned. Consider cervical spine X-ray if hardware displacement suspected.

clinical_note:
Requesting ultrasound pelvis 

64 yo female
3+ months of unilateral leg swelling. Occuring daily. normal d dimer. Nil signs of DVT. lower abdo exam. ? enlarged uterus with uterine pressure as cause of swelling. Please check for ? uterine enlargement, fibroids, other causes of pressure in leg. 

Date: 09/02/2026 10:49:59 Subjective:
- Unilateral left leg swelling present since summer, worsens with prolonged standing
- Leg becomes tight when swollen, requires elevation for relief
- Had to increase shoe size on affected side due to swelling
- Swelling extends from ankle upwards, whole left side of body appears larger than right
- No associated leg pain, just tightness when swollen

Past Medical History:
- COPD
- Hypertension
- Previous breast cancer in 50s, ceased menstruation at that time
- Three previous caesarean sections, tubal ligation performed
- Father died of pancreatic cancer
- Currently taking candesartan, Breo inhaler, Incruse inhaler, dabigatran, metoprolol
- Minimal use of blue inhaler currently \bp 136/81
\wt 95.3kg
Objective:
- Blood pressure: 137/84 mmHg
- Weight: 95.3 kg (increased from previous 86 kg)
- Abdominal examination: mild epigastric tenderness on palpation, firmness noted around abdomen, no masses palpable
- Left leg examination: mild venous insufficiency signs, slightly more red compared to right leg, few visible veins but not varicose, 
- Left leg non-pitting odema present

Assessment:
- Unilateral left leg swelling - ?venous insufficiency vs DVT vs pelvic pathology

Plan:
- General blood tests including D-dimer to exclude DVT
- Pelvic ultrasound to exclude pelvic pathology causing leg swelling
-- If D-dimer positive, leg ultrasound to be arranged
- Continue current exercise programme including pulmonary rehabilitation and walking club
- Weight loss encouraged as main treatment for venous insufficiency

ai_verdict_summary: This presentation does not match any funded criteria for pelvic ultrasound.
ai_missing_criteria: No acute pelvic pain [p46]
No pelvic mass on examination [p46]
No post-menopausal bleeding [p46]
No abnormal uterine bleeding [p46]
No Ca-125 ≥35 units/mL documented [p46]
No primary amenorrhoea [p46]
No secondary dysmenorrhoea [p46]
No PCOS criteria [p46]
No fibroid symptoms with MHT [p46]
ai_notes: The clinical presentation is unilateral leg swelling with suspected venous insufficiency. While the GP queries uterine enlargement as a cause, there are no documented pelvic symptoms, abnormal bleeding, pelvic pain, or palpable masses that would meet funded criteria for pelvic ultrasound. The examination specifically notes 'no masses palpable' and there are no gynaecological symptoms documented.
ai_redirect: Unilateral leg swelling with normal D-dimer and no DVT signs — consider venous insufficiency management as per local guidelines. If pelvic pathology genuinely suspected, document specific pelvic symptoms or examination findings that meet ultrasound criteria.
ai_suggested_wording: 64 year old female with 3+ months unilateral left leg swelling. Post-menopausal since breast cancer treatment in 50s. Examination reveals abdominal firmness but no palpable pelvic masses. Normal D-dimer excludes DVT. Assessment: unilateral leg swelling likely venous insufficiency. Plan: conservative management with weight loss and exercise. Note: Pelvic ultrasound not indicated as no pelvic symptoms, bleeding, or masses identified on examination.

ai_verdict_summary: Ultrasound pelvis for unilateral leg swelling does not meet any funded criteria — this presentation is not an indication for pelvic ultrasound.
ai_missing_criteria: No pelvic mass palpable on examination [p46]
No vaginal bleeding documented [p46]
No pelvic pain documented [p46]
No Ca-125 result documented [p46]
No abnormal uterine bleeding documented [p46]
No symptoms suggestive of ovarian pathology documented [p46]
ai_notes: Pelvic ultrasound is not indicated for investigating unilateral leg swelling. The funded criteria for pelvic ultrasound relate to gynaecological symptoms such as bleeding, pelvic pain, or suspected masses. Leg swelling from pelvic venous compression would typically be bilateral. For unilateral leg swelling with venous insufficiency signs, vascular assessment is more appropriate.
ai_redirect: Unilateral leg swelling with normal D-dimer and venous insufficiency signs — consider vascular assessment or venous duplex ultrasound of the leg rather than pelvic ultrasound

Heavier periods, bleeding through super tampons [p46]
ai_missing_criteria: No palpable pelvic mass documented on examination [p46]
ai_notes: The note describes symptoms suggestive of endometriosis but does not document pelvic examination findings. ai_verdict_title: Referral at risk — add missing information
ai_verdict_summary: Referral meets criteria for P3 ultrasound pelvis but requires documentation of Ca-125 result.
ai_met_criteria: Abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB) — irregular periods for last few months, cycles vary between 16-30 days, heavier periods [p46]
Non-uterine causes excluded — nil vaginal discharge documented [p46]
One risk factor for EH/EC — nulliparity and older than 35 (patient is 29 with 2 kids, so this is not met) [p46]
Not responded to first-line treatment trialled for at least 3 months — TXA and naproxen prescribed in Sept 2025 but patient did not use them as symptoms resolved [p46]
ai_missing_criteria: Ca-125 result — advised urgent blood test to do CA125 but result not documented [p46]
ai_notes: Note: While the referral mentions suspicion of ovarian pathology and Ca-125 testing was advised, the P3 criteria for 'new abdominal or pelvic symptoms on a persistent or frequent basis AND Ca-125 ≥35 units/mL' requires the actual Ca-125 result to be documented. The referral currently meets P3 criteria under 'Women aged younger than 45 years with AUB who have not responded to first-line treatment trialled for at least 3 months' given that treatment was prescribed but not taken due to symptom resolution.
ai_suggested_wording: 29 year old female with abnormal uterine bleeding. Irregular periods for last few months with cycles varying between 16-30 days (previously normal 26-27 day cycles). Now experiencing heavier periods bleeding through super tampons. Associated symptoms include bad back and hip pain during menses, left thigh cramps, bloating and nausea after eating for the last month, and deep pelvic pain on intercourse. Non-uterine causes excluded with nil vaginal discharge. Negative urine pregnancy test. History of PID as teenager. Sister has endometriosis. First-line treatment with TXA and naproxen was prescribed in September 2025 but patient did not use as symptoms resolved at that time. Ca-125 result: [insert value] units/mL. Clinical suspicion of endometriosis or ovarian pathology.

clinical_note:
pelvic uss 
24  year old woman

Thank you arranging pelvic ultaround for Grace 24 years AUT student presents with six-month history of progressively worsening right-sided unilateral pelvic pain with long-standing dysmenorrhoea since menarche (age 12.5). On combined OCP � bleeding improved but ongoing significant pain pre-menstrually and during menses; periods last 7�8 days. No OCP side effects. Previous trial of progestogen-only pill caused menorrhagia.

Significant dyspareunia � unable to tolerate penetrative intercourse. No new sexual partner no STD is required as per patient. 

In our clinic when initialy presented with RLQ pain- the pelvic exam was poorly tolerated, able to insert only one finger cervix not assessable. Grace not keen on repated bimanual examiantion today. Abdomen:soft, generalised tenderness, no masses. BP:110/74 mmHg BMI-31.6

FHx: mother and sisters with painful periods; no known endometriosis.
Thank you you arranging a pelvic ultrasound for diagnsotic clarification of persistent R sided unilateral pevic pain PLEASE NOTE patient is OK for attempting TV pelvic imaging.

Pelvic floor physio to be arranged 
Gynae ref after pelvic ultrasound

ai_verdict_summary: Painless jaundice requires acute assessment via ultrasound abdomen first, not CT CAP.
ai_notes: CT CAP criteria require strong suspicion of malignancy with weight loss >5% over 3-6 months plus other features. This presentation of painless jaundice has its own acute pathway via ultrasound.
ai_redirect: Ultrasound Abdomen — Acute within 48 hours for painless jaundice without obvious cause (consider discussing with secondary care or PCRL — CT may be more appropriate) [p31]
ai_safety_alert: Painless jaundice requires urgent investigation — refer for acute ultrasound abdomen within 48 hours as first-line investigation

clinical_note:
82 year old woman 
requesting uss soft tissue lump 
Appologies for clinical details 
24 nov had USS showing A well-encapsulated lesion is identified within the superficial soft
tissue of the upper abdominal wall, at the left flank. It is
generally isoechoic and measures 2.8 x 6.6 x 3.5 cm. It is partially
compressible to probe pressure, however no cystic spaces are
identified within it. There is minimal internal vascularity.

COMMENT:
The soft tissue lesion within the abdominal wall at the left flank
is favoured to represent a lipoma, without suspicious features.. 
since then ongoing pain in area lump is soft but feel more died and getting larger in size. 
Pt is bnothered by pain and pressure of same. WOuld appreicate repeat imaging to see growth etc.

ai_verdict_summary: Follow-up of a previously diagnosed lipoma without suspicious features is not routinely funded under CRR criteria.
ai_met_criteria: Previous ultrasound documented showing soft tissue mass [p59]
ai_missing_criteria: No features meeting urgent criteria: not deep to deep fascia, not fixed, not hard, not irregular, not >5cm, not changing rapidly, not recurring after previous sarcoma excision [p59]

ai_notes: Previous ultrasound already characterized this as a lipoma without suspicious features. The criteria explicitly state that soft tissue masses with characteristic appearance of lipomas without concerning features are not routinely funded. Pain and subjective increase in size of a previously diagnosed lipoma do not meet the criteria for publicly funded follow-up imaging.
